1536.built_by: Consolidated Steel Corp., Ltd. (Orange, Texas, U.S.A.)
1536.history: Decommissioned 4 October 1946. Loaned to the United States Coast Guard and (re)commissioned by them as WDE-428 on 21 August 1951. Returned to the U.S.N. and decommissioned 23 April 1954. Reclassified as DER-328 on 21 October 1955. Recommissioned 17 September 1956. Decommissioned 1 October 1973. Stricken 1 February 1974. Sold 16 August 1974 and broken up for scrap.
commanders: Lt.Cdr. Albert Henry Nienau, USNR (13 Dec 1943 - 28 Apr 1944)
Lt.Cdr. Clement D. LeHardy, USNR (28 Apr 1944 - 18 Nov 1945)

1537.built_by: Consolidated Steel Corp., Ltd. (Orange, Texas, U.S.A.)
1537.history: On 2 August 1944, USS Fiske was part of the hunter-killer Task Group 22.6 formed around the escort carrier USS Wake Island (CVE 65) when she was hit on starboard side amidships by a Gnat homing torpedo from U-804, broke in two and sank about 800 nautical miles east of Cape Race, Newfoundland in position 47º11'N, 33º29'W. 33 crew members were lost. The survivors, among them the commander and 50 wounded men, were picked up by the destroyer escort USS Farquhar (DE 139) and taken to Argentia. Hit by U-boatSunk on 2 Aug 1944 by U-804 (Meyer).
commanders: T/Cdr. Robert Power Walker, USN (25 Aug 1943 - 20 Jan 1944)
Lt. John Alfred Comly, USNR (20 Jan 1944 - 2 Aug 1944)

1538.built_by: Consolidated Steel Corp., Ltd. (Orange, Texas, U.S.A.)

Lt.Cdr. Howard Carlton Duff, USNR (14 Sep 1944 - 7 Dec 1945)

1539.history: Decommissioned 15 June 1946. Loaned to the United States Coast Guard and (re)commissioned by them as WDE-434 on 29 June 1951. Returned to the U.S.N. and decommissioned 25 May 1954. Reclassified as DER-331 on 21 October 1955. Recommissioned 23 October 1956. Decommissioned at an unknown date. Stricken at an unknown date. Transferred to South Vietnam 25 September 1971 being renamed Tran Khanh Du. Tran Khanh Du was captured while undergoing an overhaul after the fall of South Vietnam.
commanders: Cdr. Irving Edward Davis, USNR (25 Jan 1944 - 27 May 1944)
Lt. James N. Clayton, USNR (27 May 1944 - )

1540.built_by: Consolidated Steel Corp., Ltd. (Orange, Texas, U.S.A.)
1540.history: USS Frederick C. Davis was participating in Operation Teardrop, a hunt for snorkel-equipped U-boats in the Western Atlantic and was part of the 4th Escort Divison, which screened the American escort carrier USS Bogue (CVE 9) in the Southern Surface Barrier. On 24 April 1945, U-546 discovered the USS Bogue about 570 miles east-southeast of Cape Race, Newfoundland and tried to attack on periscope depth, but the USS Frederick C. Davis discovered the U-boat and prepared herself for an attack. At this moment a Gnat struck forward on the port side. The ship broke in two and sank in position 43º52'N, 40º15'W. The crew abandoned ship and were picked up within three hours by the other escort destroyers of the Division, after they had sunk U-546. Hit by U-boatSunk on 24 Apr 1945 by U-546 (Just).
commanders: Lt.Cdr. Oscar William Goepner, USNR (14 Jul 1943 - 11 Dec 1943)
Lt.Cdr. Reginald Chauncey Robbins, Jr., USNR (11 Dec 1943 - ???)
Lt James Rayner Crosby, USNR (??? - 24 Apr 1945)

Buck, USNR) was irreparably damaged by German torpedo aircraft about 35 nautical miles north-east of Algiers, Algeria in position 37º03'N, 03º58'E. 17 of the crew were killed and 12 wounded Stricken 23 September 1944. Parts used to repair USS Menges (DE 320). Hulk sold on 20 June 1947 and broken up for scrap.

1564.history: On 9 March 1944 the US Coast Guard manned USS Leopold (Cmdr. Kenneth Coy Phillips, USCG) was on her second voyage and escorting the convoy CU-16, when she got an acoustic contact about 400 miles south of Iceland and turned to investigate it. But before the destroyer escort reached the U-boat, she was hit at 22.00 hours by a Gnat from U-255 and was abandoned. The vessel broke in two and remained afloat, but both parts sank early the next morning in position 58º44'N, 25º50'W. 172 died which includes all 13 officers and only 28 ratings survived who were picked up by USS Joyce (DE 317). Hit by U-boatSunk on 9 Mar 1944 by U-255 (Harms).
commanders: Kenneth Coy Phillips, USCG (18 Oct 1943 - 9 Mar 1944 (+))

Decommissioned on 1 August 1962 and again placed in service as a Group II, Naval Reserve Training Ship at Philadelphia. Stricken 23 September 1968. Sunk as a target off the coast of Newport, Rhode Island 27 June 1969.

1595.history: USS Stewart began her service operating out of Miami as a "school ship" training student officers. She escorted President Roosevelt in the presidential yacht down the Potomac River to rendezvous with USS Iowa in the Chesapeake bay for his mission to Casablanca and Tehran. In 1944, she commenced North Atlantic convoy operations, making 30 crossings with occasional enemy submarine and aircraft encounters. Heavy seas and icing conditions were frequent. Stewart moved to the Pacific theater in mid 1945, and conducted training exercises out of Pearl Harbor until the end of the war. She was decommissioned on 27 March 1946. Stricken on 1 October 1972. On 25 June 1974, Stewart was formally donated to Seawolf Park. She is the second ship named for Rear Admiral Charles Stewart who commanded another ship in the historic naval fleet, USS Constitution, from 1813 to 1815.
commanders: Blaney Calatis Turner, USNR (31 May 1943 - 18 Jan 1944)
Lt. Alvin Chesley Wilson, Jr., USNR (18 Jan 1944 - late 1945)

1663.history: USS Johnnie Hutchins was named after Johnnie who was killed in action aboard USS LST 473 in the southwest Pacific on 4 September 1943 during the assault on Lae, New Guinea. More on this can be found at this website (offsite link). Decommissioned 14 May 1946. Recommissioned 23 June 1950. Decommissioned 25 February 1958. Stricken 1 July 1972. Sold 5 February 1974 and broken up for scrap.
commanders: Lt.Cdr. Hugh Mcleod Godsey, USNR (28 Aug 1944 - Jan 1946)
